source: Rocky Linux Admin Guide, ch. 10.4 Compression utilities (pp. 191-193)

questions.q: Compress the file usr.tar with gzip.
questions.why: gzip compresses the file in place, producing usr.tar.gz and keeping the same rights and access/modification dates.
gunzip removes the .gz extension, restoring usr.tar.

questions.q: Compress the file usr.cpio with bzip2.
questions.why: bzip2 produces usr.cpio.bz2; its compression is better than gzip but takes longer.
bunzip2 removes the .bz2 extension, restoring usr.cpio.

questions.q: How does bzip2 compare with gzip?
options: bzip2 compresses better than gzip but takes longer to run
bzip2 compresses worse but is faster
questions.why: bzip2 achieves better compression than gzip at the cost of more time/CPU.

questions.q: From LEAST to MOST efficient, what is the guide's ranking of these compressors?
questions.why: Least to most efficient: compress (.Z), gzip (.gz), bzip2 (.bz2), lzip (.lz), xz (.xz) - more efficiency costs more time/CPU.

questions.q: Why is it better to back up FIRST and then compress, rather than compress during the backup?
options: Compressing during the backup lengthens backup/restore time and makes it impossible to add files afterwards
Compressing during the backup corrupts the data
questions.why: In-line compression lengthens backup and restore times and prevents appending files, so a separate compress step is preferred.
